stem: [V3-MAPPED-2026] A 32-year-old G2P1 client has just experienced spontaneous rupture of membranes (SROM). Immediately after, the fetal heart rate (FHR) drops to 70 bpm. Based on the documentation, complete the bowtie by selecting the most likely condition, the two actions the nurse should take, and the two parameters the nurse should monitor.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content: S: At 09:15, the client in Room 3 had a spontaneous rupture of membranes. The fetal heart rate immediately dropped to 70 bpm and is sustained. B: She is a 32-year-old G2P1 at 39 weeks gestation. Her last exam was 6 cm dilated, 80% effaced, and -2 station. A: My vaginal exam reveals a palpable, pulsating loop of umbilical cord anterior to the fetal head. Maternal vitals are stable. R: I need you at the bedside immediately for an emergency C-section. I am currently elevating the presenting part.

tabs.title: Vital Signs
tabs.content:
Maternal: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 88/min, Temp 37°C (98.6°F), RR 18/min.
Fetal: HR 70/min (sustained).

tabs.title: Labs
tabs.content: Complete Blood Count (CBC) and Coagulation panel are pending.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content: Ultrasound confirms single live intrauterine pregnancy. No evidence of placental abruption. Fetal heart rate tracing shows severe bradycardia. Cord prolapse suspected.

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content: General: Anxious, diaphoretic. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, palpable fetal parts. Genitourinary: Palpable pulsating umbilical cord on vaginal exam.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content: Priority: Relieve pressure on the umbilical cord, prepare for immediate delivery. Interventions: Continuous fetal monitoring, administer oxygen, position client to relieve cord pressure, ensure IV access, prepare for C-section.

tabs.title: MAR
tabs.content: Oxygen: 10 L via non-rebreather mask. IV Fluids: Lactated Ringers at 125 mL/hr.

conditionOptions.rationale: Incorrect. Placental abruption typically presents with vaginal bleeding, uterine tenderness, and hypertonic contractions. The key finding in this case is a palpable cord, not these symptoms.

Correct. The umbilical cord has descended ahead of the presenting part, causing compression. This obstructs fetal blood flow, leading to sudden, severe bradycardia immediately following rupture of membranes.
Incorrect. Early decelerations are benign, mirror contractions, and result from head compression. The FHR of 70 bpm is severe, prolonged bradycardia, not a gradual, shallow early deceleration.

actionOptions.text: Manually elevate presenting part off cord
actionOptions.rationale: Correct. Manually lifting the fetal head off the compressed umbilical cord is the priority action. This is critical to restore oxygenated blood flow to the fetus and prevent hypoxic injury.

actionOptions.text: Position client in Knee-Chest or Trendelenburg
actionOptions.rationale: Correct. This position uses gravity to shift the fetus away from the pelvis. This further relieves pressure on the prolapsed umbilical cord while preparing for emergency delivery.
Incorrect. Leopold maneuvers are used to assess fetal position and are contraindicated in this emergency. Abdominal manipulation could worsen cord compression or cause further fetal distress.
Incorrect. Pushing would cause the fetal head to descend further, increasing compression on the umbilical cord and worsening fetal hypoxia. This action is extremely dangerous and contraindicated.

parameterOptions.rationale: Correct. Continuous FHR monitoring is essential to evaluate the fetus's response to interventions. It also assesses for ongoing distress, which guides the urgency of delivery.
Correct. Assessing for cord pulsations confirms if blood is flowing through the cord. Maximizing maternal oxygenation can improve fetal oxygen saturation during this acute event.
Incorrect. Glucose monitoring is irrelevant to the acute management of an umbilical cord prolapse. This parameter is used for managing chronic conditions like gestational diabetes.
Incorrect. The Babinski sign is a neurological assessment finding for the client or neonate after birth. It has no relevance in monitoring this acute obstetric emergency.

pearls.description: Umbilical cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency requiring immediate intervention to prevent fetal hypoxia and death.
pearls.strategy: Remember the key actions: elevate the presenting part, call for help, and prepare for immediate delivery.

pearls.description: Maintaining continuous fetal heart rate monitoring is crucial to assess fetal response to interventions.
pearls.strategy: Apply a fetal heart rate monitor and closely observe for changes in the FHR pattern.

traps.description: Delaying intervention can lead to severe fetal hypoxia and neurological damage.
traps.strategy: Act quickly and efficiently. Delegate tasks to other team members to expedite care.

traps.description: Forgetting to assess cord pulsations can lead to a false sense of security if the cord is compressed.
traps.strategy: Always palpate the cord to confirm the presence of pulsations after relieving pressure on the presenting part.

mnemonics.description: Position (knee-chest or Trendelenburg), Oxygenate, IV fluids, Support, Evaluate FHR
